metadata.seed: I have to repeat one of my classes over winter break, but I have plans with family. I will lose my scholarship if i dont pass

patient.query: I have to repeat one of my classes over winter break, but I have plans with family. I will lose my scholarship if i dont pass

automatic_thoughts: I will fail if I don't pass
I can't manage both my class and family plans

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re juggling a lot of pressure right now. What evidence do you have that you’ll fail if you don’t pass that class? Are there ways you might balance the family plans and the coursework, or get some extra support?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like a lot. I guess I just think if I don’t pass this class, it’ll ruin everything. Like, I’ve always been so focused on doing well, and if I mess this up...what does that say about me? I don’t know if anyone really understands how much I’m stressing over it. Balancing everything feels impossible right now, and asking for support just feels like admitting I can't handle it.

automatic_thoughts: it’ll ruin everything
what does that say about me?
no one understands my stress
I can't handle it

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that failing could change everything for you. What evidence do you have that this would be the case? Are there other ways you could still feel capable or valued even if the outcome isn’t what you hoped?

patient.query: I guess part of me knows it isn’t the end of the world, but it just feels so important right now. Like, I’ve always identified with being “the smart one” or the one who gets good grades, and if I don’t live up to that, then what am I? It’s like I’d disappoint everyone, including myself. I don’t even know how to separate my self-worth from my grades. It’s all just so tangled up.

automatic_thoughts: It feels so important right now
If I don’t live up to that, then what am I?
I’d disappoint everyone, including myself
I don’t know how to separate my self-worth from my grades

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight on your grades. What evidence do you have that your worth is only linked to academic performance? Have there been moments when you felt proud of something that wasn’t about grades? How might you view yourself if you celebrated achievements that aren’t academic?

patient.query: I don’t know. It just feels so overwhelming. I’ve always prided myself on being the “smart one,” and now I’m terrified of not living up to that. If I don’t pass this class, it’s like, what does that say about me? I can’t shake the feeling that I’d be a disappointment, both to myself and to everyone around me. It just feels so tangled up.

automatic_thoughts: terrified of not living up to being smart
what does that say about me?
I'd be a disappointment

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure of meeting that high standard is really heavy for you. What evidence do you have that not passing would make you a disappointment? Are there other ways you might view this situation that could feel less overwhelming?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I feel the pressure mostly from my own expectations, but also from my family and friends. They always talk about success and how important it is, and I’ve just internalized that, I think. It’s hard to imagine it any other way; I just feel like if I don’t do well, everyone will see me differently. I know I should probably think about it in a less intense way, but it’s hard to shake that feeling of failure looming over me.

automatic_thoughts: if I don’t do well, everyone will see me differently
I should think about it in a less intense way
feeling of failure looming over me

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure feels very heavy and the idea that everyone will judge you if you don’t meet those expectations can feel overwhelming. What evidence do you have that people would see you differently if you didn’t meet those expectations? Can you think of any times when you felt valued or respected even when things didn’t go exactly as planned?

patient.query: I don’t know… it’s just this nagging thought that if I don't succeed, people will think I’m lazy or that I didn’t try hard enough. I guess I’ve seen friends struggle and then it feels like everyone’s talking about them differently. But I can remember a time when I messed up a group project, and my friends still valued my input and what I brought to the table. That felt good, but it’s hard to hold onto that when everything feels so high stakes right

automatic_thoughts: if I don't succeed, people will think I’m lazy
I didn’t try hard enough
everyone’s talking about them differently

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure about how others view you. What evidence do you have that people are judging you harshly, and how does that compare to the time your friends still valued your contribution? How might you balance the high stakes you feel with the reminder that you’ve already proven yourself capable in the past?

patient.query: I don't know… it’s just this nagging thought that if I don't succeed, people will think I’m lazy or that I didn’t try hard enough. I’ve seen friends struggle and then it feels like everyone talks about them differently. I remember a time when I messed up a group project, and my friends still valued my input, and that felt good. But it’s hard to hold onto that when everything feels so high stakes right now.

automatic_thoughts: if I don't succeed, people will think I’m lazy
I didn’t try hard enough
everyone talks about them differently

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried about how others view you if you don’t meet expectations. What evidence do you have that people would think you’re lazy or that you didn’t try hard enough? Are there other ways people might interpret your effort or the situation?

patient.query: I guess I don’t really have solid evidence, more just this feeling that’s stuck in my head. Sometimes I see how quickly people jump to conclusions about others based on grades or success, and I just assume I’d be next in line for that kind of judgment. But maybe I’m just scared of opening up about not doing well and showing that side of myself. It’s easier to keep up this image of being able to handle everything, even if it’s exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I’d be next in line for judgment
I’m scared of opening up about not doing well
it's easier to keep up this image

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy that worry feels, especially when you’re trying to keep up a strong image. What evidence do you have that people would actually judge you harshly if you opened up? And what evidence might suggest they would respond differently?

patient.query: It’s true that I don’t have solid evidence. I mean, I can think of moments when my friends have been supportive, and they’ve shared their struggles too. Maybe I just focus on the negative times more. I guess it’s just hard to believe that vulnerability could be met with understanding instead of judgment. I have this instinct to hide my struggles because it feels safer… but that instinct is tiring.

automatic_thoughts: I don’t have solid evidence
Maybe I just focus on the negative times more
Vulnerability could be met with judgment
Hiding my struggles feels safer

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of worry about how others might react when you open up. What kinds of moments have made you feel that judgment might come, and can you think of any times when people responded differently? How might you weigh those experiences against the supportive moments you mentioned?

patient.query: Yeah, I think back to times in school when someone would mess up or fall behind, and I could just see how people whispered or looked at them differently. It's like those moments stick with me, and I can’t help but imagine that happening to me. But there are times when I’ve seen friends open up and still feel accepted, like when we had that heart-to-heart last semester. I guess I just push those memories aside, thinking they're the exception rather than the rule. It's just easier

automatic_thoughts: imagine that happening to me
those memories are the exception rather than the rule

llm_response.response: It sounds like you notice a pattern of thinking that if you slip up, people will look down on you. What evidence do you have that this happens to you? Are there times when you’ve seen people react differently? How might you view those moments differently?
